data.title: Worthington Enterprises Non-GAAP EPS of $0.98 beats by $0.02, revenue of $378.7M beats by $29.29M
data.description:
• Worthington Enterprises press release (WOR): Q3 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.98 beats by $0.02.
  • Revenue of $378.7M (+24.4% Y/Y) beats by $29.29M.
  • Operating cash flow increased 8% to $61.9 million, while free cash flow improved 8% to $48.1 million.
  • Repurchased 100,000 common shares for $5.4 million, leaving 4,915,000 common shares available for repurchase under the company’s existing authorization.
  • Declared a quarterly dividend of $0.19 per common share payable on June 29, 2026, to shareholders of record at the close of business on June 15, 2026.

data.title: Robinhood expands credit facility to $3.25B, approves $1.5B buyback program
data.description:
• Robinhood Markets (HOOD) subsidiary Robinhood Securities, signed an amended credit agreement.
  • The revolving credit facility size increased from $2.65B to $3.25B.
  • The facility can expand up to $4.875B under certain conditions.
  • The company also approved a new $1.5B share repurchase program.
  • The buyback replaces the prior program and adds over $1.1B in additional capacity.
  • Repurchases are expected to be executed over ~three years starting Q1 2026.

data.title: Amazon enters humanoid race with Fauna Robotics deal
data.description:
Amazon (AMZN) is stepping into the humanoid robotics space through its acquisition of Fauna Robotics, according to sources cited by Bloomberg.

The deal reportedly closed last week for an undisclosed amount. 

The New York-based startup is developing a human-like, 42-inch-tall robot called Sprout, intended for small chores like picking up things off the floor and retrieving items throughout the home. Sprout can interact with people, walk, dance, open doors, and respond to its name. The robot is also designed to hold back-and-forth conversations and display interactive social behaviors. Powered by NVIDIA’s (NVDA) Jetson Orin robotics platform, Sprout can also form memories over time.

 

 

Sprout is currently priced at $50K, including software, grippers, and a swappable battery that can last around 3 hours on a single charge.

Fauna will be integrated into Amazon’s Personal Robotics Group under the name “Fauna, an Amazon company.” Co-founders Rob Cochran and Josh Merel will remain at the company, moving operations to an Amazon location in New York.

Unlike Amazon’s (AMZN) earlier push into robotics with Astro in 2021, Sprout is being developed specifically for the consumer market.

data.title: GameStop Non-GAAP EPS of $0.49, revenue of $1.1B
data.description:
• GameStop press release (GME): Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.49.
  • Revenue of $1.1B (-14.1% Y/Y).
  • Selling, general and administrative (“SG&A”) expenses were $241.5 million for the fourth quarter, compared to $282.5 million in the prior year's fourth quarter.
  • Operating income was $135.2 million for the fourth quarter, compared to $79.8 million in the prior year's fourth quarter.
    • Excluding impairment and other items, adjusted operating income was $147.7 million for the fourth quarter, compared to $84.4 million in the prior year's fourth quarter.
  • Net income was $127.9 million for the fourth quarter, compared to $131.3 million in the prior year’s fourth quarter.
    • Excluding impairment, loss on digital assets and related receivables, and other items, adjusted net income was $291.4 million for the fourth quarter, compared to $136.4 million for the prior year's fourth quarter.
  • Cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities were $9.0 billion at the close of the quarter compared to $4.8 billion at the close of the prior year's fourth quarter.
  • Bitcoin and related receivables were valued at $368.4 million at the close of the quarter.

  • FULL YEAR OVERVIEW

    • Net sales were $3.630 billion for fiscal year 2025, compared to $3.823 billion in fiscal year 2024.
    • SG&A expenses were $910.2 million for fiscal year 2025, compared to $1.130 billion in fiscal year 2024.
    • Operating income was $232.1 million for fiscal year 2025, compared to an operating loss of $26.2 million in fiscal year 2024.
      • Excluding impairment and other items, adjusted operating income was $289.5 million for fiscal year 2025, compared to an adjusted operating loss of $26.8 million in fiscal year 2024.
    • Net income was $418.4 million for fiscal year 2025, compared to $131.3 million in fiscal year 2024.
      • Excluding impairment, loss on digital assets and related receivables, non cash interest expense related to the issuance of warrants to Convertible Noteholders, and other items, adjusted net income was $647.4 million for fiscal year 2025, compared to $131.2 million in fiscal year 2024.
  • Shares -3.77% AH.

data.title: Lenz outlines 2026 acceleration strategy while expanding VIZZ sales force to 117 reps
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: LENZ Therapeutics, Inc. (LENZ) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Evert Schimmelpennink, President and CEO, detailed the early success of LENZ's VIZZ launch, emphasizing, "the product clearly works," and highlighted that over 45,000 boxes have been sold, prescribed by more than 10,000 eye care professionals. Schimmelpennink noted, "the number of prescribing doctors we have seen at this stage of the launch already significantly exceeds what we observed with several recent eye care product launches." He stressed that the company is "building a new treatment category" and is prioritizing the foundation for scaling adoption, supported by "significant financial strength," ending the quarter with over $292 million in cash.
  • Schimmelpennink described a focus on operational execution, stating, "we are expanding our field presence to a total field organization of 117 reps," to "increase call frequency with existing prescribers while also expanding the number of physicians we actively cover."
  • CFO Daniel Chevallard reported, "we continue in the launch of VIZZ from a position of financial strength, ending 2025 with approximately $292.3 million in cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities," and added, "our Q4 results were highlighted by net product revenues of approximately $1.6 million in our first quarter of product launch with over 20,000 monthly paid and filled prescriptions."
  • Chief Commercial Officer Shawn Olsson highlighted, "aided awareness is already at 98% and unaided awareness is already at 79%" among eye care professionals, and noted, "we expect to have over 10,000 prescribing ECPs by the end of Q1, which exceeds what we observed with several recent eye care product launches."

Outlook

  • Schimmelpennink stated, "the consumer awareness engine is now beginning to build momentum, while in parallel, we continue to focus on helping physicians integrate this more consistently into their patient discussions." He emphasized that the company expects "a more meaningful impact on script volume as we move into the second half of the year," with the direct-to-consumer campaign anticipated to take at least two quarters to translate into prescription trends.
  • Chevallard indicated, "Our Q4 2025 cash burn was substantially in line with our go-forward quarterly expectations over 2026. The recently initiated sales force expansion was in our 2026 operating plan."

Financial Results

  • LENZ reported net product revenues of approximately $1.6 million for Q4 2025 with over 20,000 monthly paid and filled prescriptions.
  • The company ended 2025 with $292.3 million in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities, and remains debt-free with approximately 31.3 million shares outstanding.
  • Total Q4 operating expenses were approximately $40 million, including $4 million of noncash stock-based compensation expense, compared to $31.4 million in Q3. Net cash burn in the fourth quarter was approximately $32 million.
  • SG&A expenses in Q4 rose to $39.6 million, largely due to the establishment of the sales force and launch investments, with about 80% allocated to sales and marketing.
  • R&D expenses dropped to 0 in Q4 2025 as the company concluded key clinical studies and shifted to commercialization.
  • Chevallard noted a net loss per share of $1.16 in Q4 2025 compared to $0.46 in Q4 2024.
  • The company continues to expand global licensing, with five ex-U.S. NDA or equivalent submissions completed and over ten anticipated by year-end.

Q&A

  • Stacy Ku, TD Cowen: Asked for an update on sampling dynamics, refills, and investments to broaden patient demand. Schimmelpennink responded, "new patient starts are developing as expected for a new category that takes a little time," and Olsson added the company is "being much more focused with targeting each individual that we want to drive in versus driving a shotgun approach."
  • Yigal Nochomovitz, Citigroup: Inquired about top prescriber behaviors and communication strategies. Schimmelpennink said, "they figured out how to, one, bring this up consistently and easily and quickly and offer it as an alternative option to their patients, setting the right expectations." Olsson elaborated on optimized sampling and the need to educate about the product's broader applicability.
  • Alyssa for Marc Goodman, Leerink Partners: Asked about patient segments and distribution channels. Olsson stated, "about half of the patients on this have worn contacts. About 1/3 of them have had LASIK in the past," and noted that both e-pharmacy and retail pharmacy channels are meaningful but did not disclose the mix.
  • Biren Amin, Piper Sandler: Asked about pack size splits and refill projections. Schimmelpennink said, "we're encouraged by what we're seeing," but noted that actual refill percentages would be communicated in the second half of the year.
  • Melanie for Jason Gerberry, Bank of America: Asked about DTC program indicators. Olsson emphasized, "we're seeing up to a 10x increase in web visitors depending on what we're running," and the company focuses on driving awareness and responsible financial spend.
  • Lachlan Hanbury-Brown, William Blair: Asked about sampling volume and prescriber repeat behavior. Schimmelpennink noted, "more than 90% of people that start with this or new patients have had a sample first," and described the company's focus on building repeat prescriptions by sharing successful prescriber blueprints.
  • Matthew Caufield, H.C. Wainwright: Asked about nuances in initial refill patients. Olsson explained, "when we break it down, it's 60% women, 40% men. Predominantly that 55- to 64-year-old, there are certain states that we see perform better than others."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly positive, with questions focusing on operational details, sampling strategy, refill persistence, and marketing efficiency.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in both prepared remarks and responses, using phrases such as "we feel very good about where we are" and "what we're seeing is encouraging."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained upbeat, but shifted from launch celebration to a focus on execution and scaling adoption.
  • Analysts' sentiment in both quarters was primarily inquisitive, with a greater focus this quarter on tracking metrics and operational execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance and strategic focus in Q4 shifted toward accelerating adoption, expanding the sales force, and building consumer awareness, whereas Q3 centered on launch preparation and initial physician engagement.
  • Key metric changes include the jump from over 5,000 paid scripts in October (Q3) to over 45,000 boxes sold by the end of Q4, and growth from 2,500 to more than 10,000 prescribing doctors.
  • Management's confidence level remained high, with a greater emphasis this quarter on operational scaling, sales force expansion, and the long-term durability of the patient base.
  • Analysts moved from launch-specific queries in Q3 to operational performance and growth levers in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Schimmelpennink acknowledged, "the pace at which new patients are coming on to therapy is therefore developing more steadily," reflecting the challenge of building a new treatment category and the need for focused physician education.
  • Management identified the need to change the mindset of physicians who primarily think of the product for early presbyopes, emphasizing ongoing training and messaging refinement.
  • Analysts raised concerns about refill rates, DTC campaign effectiveness, and the speed of adoption, all met with management's assurance of ongoing monitoring and iterative optimization.

Final Takeaway

LENZ Therapeutics concluded the quarter with strong physician adoption, encouraging early patient persistence, and a robust financial position to support continued investment in VIZZ and category expansion. The company is accelerating operational efforts, expanding its sales force to 117 representatives, and increasing consumer marketing, aiming for a more pronounced impact on prescription growth in the second half of 2026. Management remains confident in the foundation laid for durable growth as physician habits and consumer awareness continue to build momentum.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Paranovus announces $5M registered direct offering
data.description:
• Paranovus Entertainment Technology (PAVS) entered a definitive agreement for a registered direct offering with an institutional investor.
  • The offering includes 14.29M Class A ordinary shares (or equivalents) priced at $0.35/share.
  • Total gross proceeds are expected to be ~$5M before fees and expenses.
  • The deal is priced at-the-market under Nasdaq rules.
  • The transaction is expected to close on or about March 25, 2026.
  • Proceeds will be used for working capital and general corporate purposes.
  • PAVS shares up 22%.

data.title: Trump: Iran offered the U.S. a ‘present’ to show good faith in war resolution negotiations
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump announced that Iran has offered the U.S. a “present” as a show of good faith amid ongoing negotiations to end a 25-day conflict that has upended global markets.

Trump declined to detail the gift, which he described as “worth a tremendous amount of money,” but confirmed it was related to energy flows through the Strait of Hormuz, with Iran’s Tasnim news agency reporting that a Thai ship passed through the vital waterway on Tuesday.

The negotiations involve a high-profile team including Vice President JD Vance, Secretary of State Marco Rubio, and special envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner.

Trump’s diplomatic optimism stood in contrast to the Pentagon’s simultaneous deployment of additional ground troops to the region.

“They’re talking to us, and they’re talking sense. It all starts with they cannot have a nuclear weapon,” Trump told reporters at the White House.

The president claimed Iran is eager to reach an agreement, asserting that the two sides have already agreed on some parameters of a potential deal.

“They want to make a deal so badly, you have no idea how badly they want to make it,” Trump said, adding his bold declaration: “We’ve won this war.”

When asked about finalizing an agreement, Trump expressed cautious optimism while acknowledging uncertainty remains.

“I think we’re going to end it; I can’t tell you for sure,” he said, reiterating that preventing Iran from obtaining nuclear weapons remains the non-negotiable starting point of any deal.

data.title: BASF raised to Buy at Deutsche Bank, seeing net benefit from Middle East war
data.description:
BASF (BASFY) (BFFAF) up 3.3% in Tuesday's trading as Deutsche Bank upgraded the world's largest chemicals producer to Buy from Hold with a €55 price target, believing the company will see net benefits from the Middle East conflict, with increasing spreads and utilization in the European and U.S. upstream businesses, which should more than offset demand and cost headwinds in parts of the business.

Specifically, global oversupply and cheap import pressure from Asia/China have been a key headwind for BASF (BASFY) (BFFAF) which should ease, Deutsche Bank analysts led by Virginie Boucher-Ferte said, raising the bank's estimated 2026-2029 forecasts for earnings per share by 19%-23% and EBITDA by 8%-10%, now 10% above consensus 2026 EBITDA.

BASF (BASFY) (BFFAF) shares trade at 7.3x estimated 2026 EV/EBITDA, below its last 10-year average, and offer a 5% dividend yield with a €1.5B buyback currently ongoing with ~€700M remaining, Boucher-Ferte wrote, adding the stock continues to trade at a premium to peers, but this has been the case for some time now, due to higher liquidity and superior positioning relative to European peers.

"We anticipate that this conflict will have relatively long-lasting consequences for European chemical companies, both negative (cost and demand headwinds) and positive (reduced oversupply and competitive pressures), even if a resolution were to occur in the near future," Boucher-Ferte wrote, adding that at the current stage, "it is impossible to definitively assess the net impact of the situation, and a wide range of outcomes remain possible."

data.title: Carlyle’s Jeff Currie on why the U.S. will be the last to feel energy disruptions
data.description:
The U.S. will be the last region to feel the impact of global energy disruptions stemming from tensions in Iran, according to Jeff Currie, partner and chief strategy officer of energy pathways at Carlyle.

Speaking at the CERAWeek conference in an interview with CNBC, Currie emphasized that “the physical realities will be what dominates this market” regardless of attempts to manipulate prices through speculation or jawboning.

Currie drew a sharp distinction between oil and traditional financial instruments, explaining that commodities cannot be easily manipulated because they must reflect current supply and demand conditions.

“It is not a financial instrument. It does not price expectations. It is a spot instrument that has to clear today’s supply and demand,” Currie said.

This fundamental characteristic means that while financial markets are anticipatory, commodity prices respond directly to physical market conditions.

The gap between Western and Eastern oil prices already reflects these physical realities. While WTI (CL1:COM) trades at $92 and Brent (CO1:COM) at $100, Currie noted that oil traded in Asia, such as Oman crude, is already fetching $150 to $160 a barrel.

In some regions, jet fuel has surpassed $300 a barrel. “In places like the Philippines, it’s already there. New Zealand, Australia—they're already feeling it,” he said.

Currie described an “air pocket” moving through global supply chains that will reach different regions at different times. With shipping from the Gulf taking three to four weeks to reach Rotterdam or the U.S. East Coast, the physical disruptions that began in early March are expected to hit European and American markets around early April. Asia, with only a two-to-three-day buffer, will feel the impact within days.

The U.S. enjoys relative insulation because it “produces what it consumes,” making Oklahoma-based WTI the last benchmark to reflect global disruptions.

However, Currie warned that American prices will begin rising once Europe starts experiencing shortages. When asked whether oil prices have already peaked and might retreat to the $70-$90 range, Currie was emphatic: “Absolutely not.”

Currie explained that peak prices will only arrive when physical shortages fully materialize.

“We have not seen peak physical strength in these markets or the shortages yet,” he said. “When that shortage hits, that’s when you’re going to see peak prices.”

Oil ETFs: (USO), (UCO), (DBO), (OILK), and (USL).

Natural Gas ETFs: (UNG), (BOIL), and (UNL).
